title: Origins of cells
summary: How could the first living cell have assembled itself from non-living chemistry? This lesson builds the full A2.1 story as a chain of reasoning, not a list of facts: the conditions on early Earth, the abiotic (spontaneous) synthesis of organic molecules shown by Miller–Urey-type experiments, the polymerisation of those monomers, the self-assembly of vesicles into protocells and why a boundary membrane matters, RNA as a plausible first self-replicating and catalytic molecule (the RNA world), the last universal common ancestor (LUCA) and the evidence for a single common origin, and — as context — the endosymbiotic theory for mitochondria and chloroplasts. The model answer is laid out exactly the way our marking engine awards Paper 2 points, so you can see how specific evidence protects your marks.
learningObjectives: Describe the conditions on early Earth and explain how organic molecules could form abiotically (spontaneously) from inorganic precursors, using Miller–Urey-type experiments as evidence.
Outline how monomers polymerise into biological polymers, and how lipids self-assemble into vesicles that model the first protocells.
Explain the significance of a boundary membrane in concentrating reactants and maintaining internal conditions distinct from the surroundings.
Explain why RNA is a strong candidate for the first self-replicating, catalytic molecule (the RNA world hypothesis).
State what the last universal common ancestor (LUCA) is, and outline the evidence for a single common origin of life; outline the endosymbiotic theory as context for eukaryotic organelles.
levelNote: Common to SL and HL at this level.

simpleExplanation.title: From simple chemistry to the first cell
simpleExplanation.summary: Life is thought to have begun when ordinary chemistry on early Earth produced organic molecules, those molecules linked into polymers, some polymers began copying themselves, and a fatty membrane wrapped the whole system into a self-contained unit. Every stage has to be plausible from what we know of chemistry and geology, and every stage has some experimental or comparative evidence behind it.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of building a working factory from raw ore. First the ore is smelted into usable parts — nuts, bolts, wire (organic monomers from inorganic matter). Then the parts are joined into machines (polymers). One machine turns out to be able to build copies of itself from the parts lying around (a self-replicating molecule). Finally a wall is thrown up around the machines, keeping the useful parts in and concentrated and the rubbish out (a boundary membrane forming a protocell). The wall is what turns a scattered pile of chemistry into a factory that can run, compete and be selected.
steps: Early Earth had no free oxygen and plenty of energy — lightning, ultraviolet light, volcanic and hydrothermal heat — driving reactions among simple inorganic molecules.
Simple organic monomers (amino acids, nucleotides, sugars) formed spontaneously; the Miller–Urey experiment showed this can happen under early-Earth-like conditions.
Monomers polymerised into chains — on hot mineral or clay surfaces, or at hydrothermal vents — giving early proteins and nucleic acids.
RNA, able both to store information and to catalyse reactions, could copy itself — an 'RNA world' preceding DNA and protein enzymes.

Lipids self-assembled into vesicles; when one enclosed self-replicating molecules it became a protocell, with an inside chemically different from its surroundings and able to undergo natural selection.

flashcards.back: The hypothesis that life arose ONCE from non-living matter through ordinary chemical and physical processes over a long time. It is a claim about a unique historical event, not an everyday occurrence.

Abiogenesis = the original, gradual, one-off origin of simple life from non-life on early Earth. Spontaneous generation = the DISPROVEN idea that complex organisms (maggots, mice) appear routinely from non-living matter. Do not confuse the two.

A REDUCING atmosphere with little or no free O₂ (which would otherwise oxidise organic molecules), abundant energy from lightning, UV, volcanism and hydrothermal heat, and inorganic precursors such as CH₄, NH₃, H₂ and H₂O.

A sealed apparatus simulating early-Earth conditions: a mixture of methane, ammonia, hydrogen and water vapour with electrical sparks for lightning. It produced amino acids and other organics, showing organic molecules can form ABIOTICALLY from inorganic precursors.

Joining monomers into polymers by condensation is difficult in bulk water. Proposed sites: hot, drying mineral/clay surfaces that concentrate monomers and catalyse bonding, and hydrothermal vents supplying energy and gradients.

A spherical compartment bounded by a lipid bilayer or monolayer that forms SPONTANEOUSLY when amphipathic lipids are placed in water. Vesicles are the experimental model for how a boundary membrane could arise before cells.

A membrane-bound droplet enclosing self-replicating molecules, with an internal chemistry distinct from its surroundings. A hypothetical precursor to true cells that can grow, divide and be subject to natural selection.

It (1) concentrates reactants together so reactions proceed, (2) keeps products and information molecules from diffusing away, (3) maintains internal conditions different from outside, and (4) makes a discrete unit that can be selected — one membrane, one 'individual'.

The idea that RNA — not DNA or protein — was the first self-replicating molecule, because RNA can BOTH store information in its base sequence AND fold to catalyse reactions. It resolves the DNA-versus-protein 'chicken-and-egg' problem.
An RNA molecule that acts as a catalyst. Ribozymes (including the catalytic core of the ribosome) are living evidence that RNA can do enzyme-like work, supporting the RNA world.

flashcards.front: Why RNA before DNA and protein
flashcards.back: DNA stores information but cannot catalyse; proteins catalyse but cannot template their own copying. RNA does a bit of both, so a single molecule type could carry information AND copy it — no other component needed first.

The most recent POPULATION of organisms from which all life today descends. LUCA is not the first cell or the first molecule — earlier life existed; LUCA is simply the common ancestor everything living traces back to.

flashcards.front: Evidence for a single common origin
flashcards.back: The (near-)universal genetic code, the shared use of DNA→RNA→protein, ATP as energy currency, the same 20 L-amino acids and the same core biochemistry across all domains all point to one common ancestor rather than many independent origins.

Mitochondria and chloroplasts arose when a host cell engulfed free-living prokaryotes that were retained rather than digested. Evidence: their own circular DNA, 70S (prokaryote-type) ribosomes, double membranes and binary-fission-like division.

quickCheck.prompt: Miller–Urey-type experiments are evidence for which claim?
options: That complex animals can arise from decaying matter today
That organic molecules can form spontaneously from inorganic precursors under early-Earth conditions
That RNA is older than DNA
That the first cells had mitochondria
quickCheck.answer: That organic molecules can form spontaneously from inorganic precursors under early-Earth conditions

quickCheck.prompt: Which is the single most important function of the boundary membrane in a protocell?
options: It makes energy for the cell
It stores the genetic code
It concentrates reactants and maintains an internal environment different from the surroundings
It catalyses the polymerisation of amino acids
quickCheck.answer: It concentrates reactants and maintains an internal environment different from the surroundings

quickCheck.prompt: Why is RNA, rather than DNA or protein, proposed as the first self-replicating molecule?
options: Because RNA is more chemically stable than DNA for long-term storage
Because RNA can both store information and catalyse reactions, so one molecule can do both jobs
Because RNA is found only in modern cells
Because RNA cannot be copied and so never changes
quickCheck.answer: Because RNA can both store information and catalyse reactions, so one molecule can do both jobs

quickCheck.prompt: Which statement about LUCA is correct?
options: LUCA was the first molecule capable of self-replication
LUCA was the very first cell ever to exist
LUCA is a population of organisms from which all present-day life descends
LUCA is the ancestor only of prokaryotes
quickCheck.answer: LUCA is a population of organisms from which all present-day life descends

quickCheck.prompt: The near-universal genetic code shared by bacteria, archaea and eukaryotes is best explained by:
options: Life arising independently many separate times
Descent of all life from a common ancestor
Spontaneous generation occurring continuously
Mitochondria being engulfed by an early cell
quickCheck.answer: Descent of all life from a common ancestor

faq.q: What is the difference between abiogenesis and spontaneous generation?
faq.a: Abiogenesis is the scientific hypothesis for the ORIGINAL formation of simple life from non-living matter, occurring once over millions of years on early Earth through ordinary chemistry. Spontaneous generation was the older, experimentally disproven idea that complex organisms — maggots from meat, mice from grain — arise routinely from non-living matter in days. Confusing the two is a classic error: abiogenesis concerns a unique historical event and simple chemistry, not the everyday appearance of complex organisms.

faq.q: Does modern understanding still support the Miller–Urey experiment?
faq.a: Yes, in its core conclusion. Geologists now think the early atmosphere may have been less strongly reducing than Miller and Urey assumed, but the essential result stands: organic building blocks such as amino acids form spontaneously from simple inorganic molecules when energy is supplied. Later experiments using different gas mixtures, and organics found on meteorites and at hydrothermal vents, all reinforce that abiotic synthesis is chemically plausible.

faq.q: Why does the origin of a boundary membrane matter so much?
faq.a: Without a boundary, useful molecules simply diffuse away and reactions never build up. A membrane concentrates reactants together, keeps information molecules and their products in one place, and holds an internal chemistry different from the surroundings. Crucially it also creates a discrete individual — one membrane, one unit — that can grow, divide and be favoured or eliminated by natural selection. Chemistry alone becomes biology once it is packaged.

faq.q: What exactly is LUCA, and is it the first cell?
faq.a: LUCA is the last universal common ancestor: the most recent population of organisms from which every living thing today descends. It is NOT the first cell or the first self-replicating molecule — life almost certainly existed before LUCA, but those earlier lineages either died out or are not ancestral to us. LUCA is simply the shared ancestor everything alive traces back to, inferred from the biochemistry all modern life has in common.

faq.q: How does endosymbiotic theory fit into the origin of cells?
faq.a: It explains a later step: how eukaryotic cells acquired mitochondria and chloroplasts. A host cell engulfed free-living aerobic bacteria (and later photosynthetic cyanobacteria) and kept them alive as partners rather than digesting them. The evidence is striking — these organelles have their own circular DNA, prokaryote-type 70S ribosomes, double membranes and divide by a fission-like process. It is context for A2.1 rather than the origin of the very first cell, but it shows the same theme: cells built from simpler living units.

sections.content: A planet with no life somehow gave rise to the first cell. That transition is the subject of A2.1, and the scientific case for it is not a single proven fact but a CHAIN OF REASONING in which each link is chemically plausible and supported by evidence: the conditions on early Earth, the spontaneous formation of organic molecules, their polymerisation, the self-assembly of a membrane, a molecule that could copy itself, and finally a common ancestor whose fingerprint every living thing still carries. This lesson works through that chain in order, and because A2.1 is examined largely through 'outline' and 'explain' questions, the model answer at the end is set out exactly the way our marking engine awards the marks — so you can see why specific evidence beats a vague 'life just happened'.
Conditions on early Earth

Life is thought to have appeared within a few hundred million years of Earth's formation, on a world utterly unlike today's. There was essentially NO free oxygen — the atmosphere was reducing — which matters because oxygen readily oxidises and destroys fragile organic molecules, so their absence gave newly formed organics a chance to persist. Energy was abundant and varied: frequent lightning, intense ultraviolet radiation reaching an unshielded surface, volcanic activity and the heat and chemical gradients of deep-sea hydrothermal vents. The raw materials were simple inorganic molecules such as water, methane, ammonia, hydrogen, carbon dioxide and dissolved minerals. In the 1920s Oparin and Haldane independently proposed that under exactly these conditions, energy could drive simple inorganic molecules to react and accumulate as organic compounds in the oceans — the so-called 'primordial soup'.

items: **Reducing atmosphere:** little or no free O₂, so organic molecules were not immediately oxidised and destroyed.
**Abundant energy:** lightning, ultraviolet radiation, volcanic heat and hydrothermal vents drove otherwise-unfavourable reactions.
**Inorganic precursors:** water, methane, ammonia, hydrogen, carbon dioxide and dissolved minerals were available.
**Oparin–Haldane hypothesis:** these conditions could produce and accumulate organic molecules in a 'primordial soup'.

sections.content: Abiotic (spontaneous) formation of organic molecules

The first testable prediction of the Oparin–Haldane idea is that organic molecules can form ABIOTICALLY — spontaneously, from non-living matter — given early-Earth conditions and an energy source. In 1953 Miller and Urey built an apparatus to test this. A flask of water (the ocean) was heated to circulate vapour into a chamber containing methane, ammonia and hydrogen (a reducing atmosphere), where electrical sparks simulated lightning. After the mixture had circulated for about a week, they analysed the condensed liquid and found several amino acids — the monomers of proteins — along with other organic molecules, all built from purely inorganic starting materials. This was the key experimental support for the claim that life's building blocks could arise from non-life through ordinary chemistry.

It is vital to state what this experiment does and does not show. It demonstrates ABIOGENESIS as a plausible chemical process — the one-off, gradual origin of organic molecules from inorganic matter. It has nothing to do with SPONTANEOUS GENERATION, the disproven idea that complex organisms appear routinely from decaying matter. Miller–Urey makes simple monomers, not organisms; the relevance is that if amino acids and nucleotide components form so readily, the raw materials for life were probably plentiful on early Earth. Additional support comes from organic molecules, including amino acids, detected on meteorites, showing such chemistry is not even unique to Earth.

items: **Prediction tested:** organic monomers can form spontaneously from inorganic precursors plus energy.
**Method:** CH₄ + NH₃ + H₂ + H₂O vapour circulated past electrical sparks (simulated lightning).
**Result:** amino acids and other organic molecules were produced from inorganic matter.
**Interpretation:** supports ABIOGENESIS (a one-off chemical origin), NOT spontaneous generation of organisms.
**Corroboration:** amino acids found on meteorites show abiotic synthesis occurs beyond Earth too.

sections.question: A student repeats a Miller–Urey-type experiment. The reaction vessel begins with 100 mmol of methane (CH₄). After one week the condensed liquid is found to contain 2.5 mmol of the amino acid glycine (C₂H₅NO₂). Assuming all the carbon in the glycine came from the methane, calculate the percentage of the methane carbon that ended up in glycine, and comment on what the result shows. [4]
sections.solution:
**Step 1 — carbon supplied by methane.**
Each CH₄ molecule contains 1 carbon atom, so 100 mmol CH₄ provides 100 mmol of carbon. [M1: initial moles of C]

**Step 2 — carbon captured in glycine.**
Each glycine (C₂H₅NO₂) contains 2 carbon atoms, so 2.5 mmol glycine contains 2 × 2.5 = 5.0 mmol of carbon. [M1: moles of C in product]

**Step 3 — percentage conversion.**
percentage = (carbon in glycine ÷ initial carbon) × 100 = (5.0 ÷ 100) × 100 = 5.0%. [A1: 5.0%]

**Step 4 — comment.**
Even a small percentage yield (5.0%) from a single simple energy source in one week shows that amino acids form readily and abiotically from inorganic precursors; over geological timescales and many energy sources such molecules could accumulate substantially. [A1: valid interpretation]

*Note the reasoning: the mark is for connecting the number to the abiotic-synthesis conclusion, not merely stating 5.0%. If your Step 2 value were wrong, error-carried-forward (ECF) would still credit a correctly calculated percentage from it.*

sections.content: Polymerisation into biological polymers

Monomers alone are not life; they must join into POLYMERS — amino acids into polypeptides, nucleotides into nucleic acids. This is a problem, because linking monomers is a condensation reaction that releases water, and doing it in the middle of a watery ocean is thermodynamically unfavourable (the surrounding water pushes the reaction backwards). Two settings are proposed to overcome this. First, hot, drying MINERAL or CLAY surfaces: as shallow pools evaporate, monomers become concentrated and adsorbed onto charged clay particles, which hold them close together and can catalyse bond formation. Second, HYDROTHERMAL VENTS, where mineral-lined pores supply both the energy and the confined, gradient-rich microenvironments in which chains can grow. In both cases the trick is the same — concentrate the monomers and remove or exclude bulk water so that polymerisation can proceed.

items: Polymerisation is a condensation reaction, unfavourable in bulk water because it releases water.
**Drying clay/mineral surfaces:** evaporation concentrates monomers; charged surfaces adsorb and catalyse bonding.
**Hydrothermal vents:** confined mineral pores supply energy and gradients that favour chain growth.
The common requirement is to concentrate monomers and exclude bulk water.

sections.content: Vesicles, protocells and the significance of a boundary membrane

A pool of polymers is still just chemistry spread through the water. The step that makes it biology is a BOUNDARY. Fatty acids and phospholipids are amphipathic — a water-attracting head and a water-repelling tail — so in water they self-assemble, with no outside help, into micelles and bilayer VESICLES: hollow spheres enclosing a droplet of solution. This is easily demonstrated in the laboratory, which is why vesicles are the standard model for how the first membrane could have appeared. A vesicle that happened to enclose self-replicating molecules is a PROTOCELL: a discrete unit whose inside is chemically different from the outside.

Why does the membrane matter so much? Four reasons, and examiners want the specific ones. (1) It CONCENTRATES reactants together in a tiny volume, so reactions that would be impossibly dilute in the open ocean can actually proceed. (2) It RETAINS the information molecules and their products, so a molecule that copies itself keeps its copies nearby rather than losing them to diffusion. (3) It MAINTAINS internal conditions — pH, ion concentrations, useful intermediates — distinct from and more favourable than the surroundings. (4) It creates a DISCRETE INDIVIDUAL: one membrane defines one unit that can grow, divide and be acted on by natural selection, so protocells that replicated faster or held their contents better would come to dominate. In short, the membrane is what turns a smear of reactions into a competing, evolving entity.

items: Amphipathic lipids self-assemble in water into bilayer VESICLES with no external input.
A vesicle enclosing self-replicating molecules is a PROTOCELL, with an inside chemically distinct from outside.
**Concentrate:** reactants are held together so reactions can proceed.
**Retain:** information molecules and products do not diffuse away.
**Maintain:** internal pH, ions and intermediates differ from the surroundings.
**Individualise:** one membrane = one unit that can grow, divide and undergo natural selection.

sections.content: RNA as the first self-replicating and catalytic molecule

A protocell only becomes alive in any meaningful sense once something inside it can COPY itself and pass on information. In modern cells this job is split: DNA stores the information, and protein enzymes carry out catalysis, including the copying of DNA. That split creates a 'chicken-and-egg' problem for origins — you need enzymes to copy the genetic material, but you need the genetic material to specify the enzymes, so which came first? The RNA WORLD hypothesis dissolves the paradox. RNA can do BOTH jobs: like DNA it stores information in its base sequence, and like a protein it can fold into a precise shape and CATALYSE reactions. RNA catalysts are not hypothetical — they exist today as RIBOZYMES, and the catalytic heart of the ribosome (which builds every protein you make) is itself RNA. So a single type of molecule, RNA, could have both held information and catalysed its own replication, with DNA (more stable storage) and proteins (more versatile catalysis) evolving later as specialists. This is why RNA is placed BEFORE DNA and protein in the origin of life, not after.

items: Modern cells split roles: DNA = information store, protein enzymes = catalysis — a 'chicken-and-egg' problem for origins.
RNA can BOTH store information (base sequence) AND catalyse reactions (fold into an active shape).
**Ribozymes** are real RNA catalysts; the ribosome's catalytic core is RNA — direct evidence RNA can do enzyme work.
One molecule doing both jobs means RNA could self-replicate without needing DNA or protein first.
DNA (stable storage) and proteins (versatile catalysis) are later specialists that took over RNA's dual role.

sections.content: LUCA and the evidence for a common origin

Trace all living things back far enough and their lineages converge on a single population: the LAST UNIVERSAL COMMON ANCESTOR, or LUCA. Be precise about what LUCA is and is not. It is NOT the first cell, and certainly not the first self-replicating molecule — life existed before LUCA, but those earlier lineages either went extinct or are not ancestral to anything alive today. LUCA is simply the most recent population from which EVERY organism now living descends: bacteria, archaea and eukaryotes all branch from it. The evidence that such a single common ancestor existed, rather than life arising many separate times, lies in the deep biochemical UNITY of life. All cells use essentially the same genetic code, the same flow of information DNA → RNA → protein, the same 20 L-amino acids, ATP as the universal energy currency, and a shared core metabolism. It would be an extraordinary coincidence for independent origins to hit on the same arbitrary code and chemistry; far more parsimonious is descent, with modification, from one common ancestor.

items: **LUCA = last universal common ancestor:** the most recent POPULATION from which all present-day life descends.
LUCA is NOT the first cell or the first molecule — earlier life existed but is not ancestral to us.
**Evidence for common origin:** near-universal genetic code, DNA→RNA→protein, 20 L-amino acids, ATP, shared core biochemistry.
Shared arbitrary features are far better explained by common descent than by many independent origins.

sections.content: Endosymbiotic theory (context)

The origin of the first cell is not the end of the story — the origin of the complex EUKARYOTIC cell involved a second, later merger of living units, and A2.1 uses it as context. The endosymbiotic theory proposes that mitochondria and chloroplasts were once free-living prokaryotes. An early host cell engulfed aerobic bacteria and, instead of digesting them, kept them as internal partners that supplied energy — these became mitochondria; a later engulfment of photosynthetic cyanobacteria gave rise to chloroplasts. The evidence is compelling and worth knowing: these organelles carry their OWN small circular DNA, have prokaryote-type 70S RIBOSOMES, are bounded by a DOUBLE MEMBRANE (the inner one their own, the outer from the host's engulfing vesicle), and reproduce by a binary-fission-like process independent of the rest of the cell. Endosymbiosis reinforces the recurring theme of A2.1: complex living things are assembled from simpler living units.

items: Mitochondria and chloroplasts were once free-living prokaryotes engulfed and retained by a host cell.
**Own circular DNA** and **70S (prokaryote-type) ribosomes** — like bacteria, unlike the host nucleus.
**Double membrane** — inner from the original prokaryote, outer from the host's engulfing vesicle.
Divide by a **binary-fission-like** process, semi-independently of the cell.

sections.content: Common mistakes examiners penalise

items: **Confusing abiogenesis with spontaneous generation** — abiogenesis is the one-off, gradual origin of SIMPLE life from non-life; spontaneous generation (complex organisms from decay, day to day) was disproven. Never describe Miller–Urey as supporting spontaneous generation.
**Saying Miller–Urey 'created life' or 'made cells'** — it produced organic MONOMERS (amino acids) from inorganic matter, evidence for abiotic synthesis only, not living organisms.

**Forgetting the reducing atmosphere** — the near-absence of free O₂ matters because oxygen would oxidise and destroy the organic molecules; leaving this out weakens the explanation of early-Earth conditions.

**Vague membrane answers** — 'the membrane protects the cell' scores little. State the specific functions: CONCENTRATE reactants, RETAIN molecules, MAINTAIN internal conditions distinct from outside, create a unit for natural selection.

**Getting the RNA-first logic backwards** — the point is that RNA can BOTH store information AND catalyse, so it can copy itself without needing DNA or protein first. Do not claim RNA came after proteins or that DNA came first.
**Misdefining LUCA** — LUCA is a POPULATION and the common ancestor of all living things, NOT the first cell and NOT the first self-replicating molecule.

**Vague 'life just happened' reasoning** — mark schemes reward SPECIFIC evidence (Miller–Urey, ribozymes, universal genetic code, organelle DNA) tied to a specific claim. A general assertion that life arose from chemistry earns nothing.

**Muddling the evidence for endosymbiosis** — cite the concrete points (own circular DNA, 70S ribosomes, double membrane, fission-like division), not a hand-wave that organelles 'used to be bacteria'.

sections.content: Model answer — marked the way our engine marks it

A2.1 is examined mostly through 'outline' and 'explain' questions, and these are marked ANALYTICALLY: each distinct valid point is worth one mark, up to the total available. Method/reasoning points and answer/conclusion points both count, equivalents are accepted, and error-carried-forward means a wrong earlier step does not automatically sink the marks that follow. The engine deliberately rewards SPECIFIC evidence and reasoning over vague statements that 'life just arose'. Study how each mark below attaches to a named, checkable idea.

sections.question: Outline the evidence and reasoning that supports the hypothesis that the first cells arose from non-living matter. [4]
sections.solution:
**Model answer.**
Early Earth had a reducing atmosphere with little free oxygen and abundant energy from lightning, UV radiation and hydrothermal vents, conditions under which organic molecules could form spontaneously from inorganic precursors. The Miller–Urey experiment supports this: sparking a mixture of methane, ammonia, hydrogen and water vapour produced amino acids, showing organic monomers can arise abiotically. These monomers could polymerise on clay or mineral surfaces or at hydrothermal vents, while amphipathic lipids self-assemble into vesicles, providing a boundary membrane that concentrates reactants and maintains an internal environment, forming protocells. RNA is a plausible first self-replicating molecule because it can both store information and act as a catalyst (as shown by ribozymes), allowing early replication without DNA or protein. Finally, the shared genetic code and common biochemistry of all life today are evidence that all cells descend from a common ancestor (LUCA), consistent with a single origin from non-living matter.

---
**How our marking engine awards the 4 marks** — each DISTINCT valid point scores 1 (maximum 4). Award any four of:

- **A (evidence):** early Earth had a reducing atmosphere / no free O₂ and abundant energy — plausible conditions for abiotic synthesis.
- **A (evidence):** Miller–Urey-type experiments produced amino acids / organic molecules from inorganic precursors — organic molecules can form abiotically.
- **A (reasoning):** monomers polymerise (on clay/mineral surfaces or at hydrothermal vents) into biological polymers.
- **A (reasoning):** lipids self-assemble into vesicles / a boundary membrane concentrates reactants and maintains internal conditions → protocells.
- **A (reasoning):** RNA could self-replicate AND catalyse (ribozymes), so it could act as the first replicator before DNA/protein.
- **A (evidence):** universal genetic code / shared biochemistry → common ancestor (LUCA) → single origin from non-life.

**Accept equivalents.** 'No free oxygen' = 'reducing atmosphere'; 'ribozyme' = 'RNA that acts as an enzyme'; 'protocell' = 'membrane-bound droplet with self-replicating contents'. Wording need not match; the IDEA must be present.

**What the engine rewards and rejects.** SPECIFIC evidence scores; vagueness does not. 'Life just arose from chemicals over time' earns ZERO — it names no condition, experiment, mechanism or comparative evidence. But 'Miller–Urey made amino acids from inorganic gases' earns a mark because it is a specific, checkable piece of evidence tied to the claim. Because six creditable points are available for a 4-mark question, there is room to miss two and still score full marks — but the points must be DISTINCT: describing Miller–Urey twice in different words scores once.

**ECF and independence.** The points stand alone, so a student who says nothing about LUCA but nails four earlier points still scores 4; and an incorrect detail in one point does not remove credit from the others.

**Bottom line:** give four distinct, specific points across the chain — early-Earth conditions, abiotic synthesis (Miller–Urey), polymerisation/membrane, RNA replication, common ancestor — and full marks follow. A single vague sentence about life 'happening' scores nothing.

sections.content: The Practice button marks YOUR answer this same way — point by point, accepting equivalents, applying ECF, and capping at the marks available. So write in distinct, creditable statements and anchor each to specific evidence: name the reducing atmosphere, name Miller–Urey and amino acids, name the membrane's job (concentrate/maintain), name RNA's dual role (ribozymes), name LUCA and the universal code. The engine rewards specific evidence over a vague 'life just happened', so make every sentence a separately markable point.
Where this leads

A2.1 sets up the whole theme of Unity and diversity. The common ancestry you meet here — one genetic code, one core biochemistry, LUCA — is the foundation for classification, phylogenetics and evolution later in the course, where DNA and protein sequences are used to reconstruct the tree of life descending from that common origin. The endosymbiotic step points forward to the structure of eukaryotic cells and their organelles. Hold on to the central habit of this topic: build the argument as a chain of plausible, evidence-backed steps, and always prefer a specific piece of evidence to a general claim.

sections.label: Get a Paper 2 question marked: outline the evidence and reasoning that the first cells arose from non-living matter

items.label: Nature Education: Hydrothermal Vents and the Origin of Life
